question: A 45 year old male had multiple hypoaesthetic mildly erythematous large plaques with elevated margins on trunk and extremities. His ulnar and lateral popliteal nerves on both sides were enlarged. The most probable diagnosis is –
vs_A_B_trace:
[
  "The presence of hypoaesthetic erythematous plaques indicates leprosy with significant sensory impairment.",
  "Large plaques with elevated margins are characteristic of borderline forms rather than lepromatous leprosy, which typically has diffuse, symmetric, poorly defined lesions.",
  "Bilateral enlargement of ulnar and lateral popliteal nerves supports a borderline-spectrum leprosy diagnosis."
]
vs_A_B_justification: Multiple hypoaesthetic erythematous plaques with elevated margins and enlarged peripheral nerves are most consistent with borderline leprosy.
vs_B_C_trace:
[
  "Large hypoaesthetic erythematous plaques with elevated margins indicate a tuberculoid-spectrum leprosy lesion.",
  "Borderline tuberculoid leprosy commonly presents with well-defined raised plaques and prominent nerve enlargement.",
  "Borderline lepromatous leprosy typically has more numerous, symmetric, less well-defined lesions with less marked sensory loss in individual plaques."
]
vs_B_C_justification: The combination of hypoaesthetic large plaques with raised margins and enlarged peripheral nerves is most consistent with borderline tuberculoid leprosy.
vs_A_C_trace:
[
  "The lesions are hypoaesthetic erythematous plaques, indicating leprosy with significant sensory impairment.",
  "Large plaques with elevated, well-defined margins are characteristic of borderline tuberculoid leprosy rather than lepromatous disease.",
  "Peripheral nerve enlargement supports tuberculoid/borderline tuberculoid spectrum disease, while lepromatous and borderline lepromatous lesions are typically more numerous, diffuse, and less sharply raised."
]
vs_A_C_justification: Large hypoaesthetic plaques with elevated margins and enlarged peripheral nerves are most consistent with borderline tuberculoid leprosy.
